results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Elsa Garmire (born November 9, 1939) is a distinguished American physicist and engineer renowned for her pioneering work in nonlinear optics and laser technology. Between 1966 and 1973, she served as a Senior Research Fellow at the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) in the laboratory of Amnon Yariv. During this period, she became deeply involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), joining the organization in 1968. Her technical expertise in lasers, holography, and light diffraction allowed her to bridge the gap between science and art; she notably contributed to the design of the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, where she helped create a massive hemispherical mirror and various light displays. Garmire is also credited with co-developing the technology and aesthetic for the first commercial laser light shows, collaborating with filmmaker Ivan Dryer to produce 'Laserium.' Her scientific career includes being the first graduate student of Nobel laureate Charles Townes at MIT, where she earned her PhD in 1965. She later became the William Hogue Professor of Electrical Engineering at the University of Southern California and served as the first female dean of the Thayer School of Engineering at Dartmouth College (1995–1997). She was elected to the National Academy of Engineering in 1989 and served as the President of the Optical Society of America in 1993.
